entry.title: Combined summary - Hourglass V2 Update
entry.summary: The Hourglass proposal, initially aimed at mitigating the risks associated with mass liquidation of P2PK funds within the Bitcoin network, has been revised following feedback from the community. The updated proposal introduces a significant change by limiting the amount of Bitcoin that can be spent from specific outputs to a maximum of one Bitcoin per block. This amendment is designed to enhance the security and stability of the Bitcoin network by preventing potential market disruptions that could occur due to large, simultaneous liquidations. Detailed information on the technical adjustments and the rationale behind these changes is available in the revised proposal document, which interested parties can access through the provided GitHub link: [Hourglass Proposal V2](https://github.com/cryptoquick/bips/blob/hourglass-v2/bip-hourglass-v2.mediawiki). The inception of the Hourglass proposal and its evolution have been marked by active participation and contributions from the Bitcoin development community. The original discussion, which sparked extensive comments and suggestions, underscores the community's proactive stance in identifying and addressing potential vulnerabilities that could affect Bitcoin's operational framework. Interested individuals can revisit these discussions for a deeper understanding of the proposal's background and the community's role in its development through the link: [Bitcoin Development Mailing List Discussion](https://groups.google.com/g/bitcoindev/c/zmg3U117aNc/m/lDCMs9j7EAAJ). This ongoing dialogue reflects an open and collaborative effort to refine the Hourglass proposal, encouraging contributors to share their insights or concerns. Such a process highlights the importance of community engagement and expert input in driving protocol developments that aim to bolster Bitcoin's resilience against threats while accommodating the needs of its diverse user base. Despite the intentions behind the Hourglass proposal to safeguard the Bitcoin network, there are voices within the community expressing opposition. Critics argue that Bitcoin should not integrate an in-protocol mechanism that restricts users' ability to spend their coins, as it could compromise the integrity and utility of Bitcoin as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system. These perspectives contribute to the broader debate on how best to balance network security with user flexibility and autonomy, underscoring the complexity of managing and evolving a decentralized digital currency.
